1.description: Impulse response: shock toA★with (Panel A) and without risk-sharing (Panel B). This figure plots the impulse response function (IRF) to a shockεA★from 2,000 model simulations. Quantities are log-deviations from their nonstochastic steady-state values. Domestic consumption and foreign consumption areCtandCt★in the model, respectively. Import penetration isIJ, the fraction of exporters isζJ, and domestic profits isπD,J. Red lines correspond to industries with low trade costs that are more exposed to foreign competition. Blue lines are industries with higher trade costs. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Impulse response: shock toA★without risk-sharing. This figure plots the Impulse Response Function (IRF) to a shockεA★from 2,000 model simulations. Quantities are log-deviations from their nonstochastic steady-state values. Domestic consumption and foreign consumption areCtandCt★in the model, respectively. Import penetration isIJ, the fraction of exporters isζJ, and domestic profits isπD,J. Red lines correspond to industries with low trade costs that are more exposed to foreign competition. Blue lines are industries with higher trade costs. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Sensitivity analysis: degree of risk-sharing. In this figure, we simulate the model for one million periods. In Panel A, we estimate average returns for different values of the risk-sharing parameter Ξ between zero and one. We include the risk-free rate to illustrate how returns exposed to trade act as a hedge with sufficient risk-sharing. In Panel B, we show the elasticity of consumption to shocks to foreign productivityA★. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
